edges.evidence_quote: Batasuna's political status was controversial. It was considered to be the political wing of ETA.
edges.context: Batasuna is considered the political wing of ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Batasuna , Herri Batasuna , FARC , FLB-ARB , Provisional IRA , Terra Lliure
edges.context: FARC is listed as an ally of ETA in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Batasuna , Herri Batasuna , FARC , FLB-ARB , Provisional IRA , Terra Lliure
edges.context: FLB-ARB is listed as an ally of ETA in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Batasuna , Herri Batasuna , FARC , FLB-ARB , Provisional IRA , Terra Lliure
edges.context: Provisional IRA is listed as an ally of ETA in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Batasuna , Herri Batasuna , FARC , FLB-ARB , Provisional IRA , Terra Lliure
edges.context: Terra Lliure is listed as an ally of ETA in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: ETA grew out of a student group called Ekin, founded in the early 1950s
edges.context: ETA originated from the student group Ekin.

edges.evidence_quote: ETA was founded on 31 July 1959 as Euskadi Ta Askatasuna by students frustrated by the moderate stance of the Basque Nationalist Party.
edges.context: ETA was founded by students frustrated with the Basque Nationalist Party's moderation.

edges.evidence_quote: The Amara station attack was claimed by the Portuguese and Galician left-wing group Directorio Revolucionario Ibérico de Liberación (DRIL)
edges.context: DRIL claimed responsibility for a bombing sometimes attributed to ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: During the 1980s, a 'dirty war' ensued using the Grupos Antiterroristas de Liberación (GAL, 'Antiterrorist Liberation Groups'), a paramilitary group which billed themselves as counter-terrorist, active between 1983 and 1987. The GAL's stated mission was to avenge every ETA killing with another killing of ETA exiles
edges.context: GAL conducted a dirty war against ETA, killing ETA members and exiles.

edges.evidence_quote: GAL activities were a follow-up of similar dirty war actions by death squads, actively supported by members of Spanish security forces and secret services, using names such as Batallón Vasco Español active from 1975 to 1981.
edges.context: Batallón Vasco Español conducted similar dirty war actions against ETA before GAL.

edges.evidence_quote: Basque parties connected to ETA such as KAS and the MLVN created a new far-left Herri Batasuna coalition
edges.context: Herri Batasuna was created by parties connected to ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: One of the parties within Herri Batasuna, ETA-affiliated KAS, listed five conditions from ETA
edges.context: KAS was an ETA-affiliated party that listed conditions for ETA to stop armed struggle.

edges.evidence_quote: ETA VII accepted this partial amnesty granted by the now democratic Spanish government and integrated into the political party Euskadiko Ezkerra
edges.context: ETA VII faction integrated into Euskadiko Ezkerra after accepting amnesty.

edges.evidence_quote: in 1998 the Catholic organisation Community of Sant'Egidio offered to negotiate with the Spanish government on behalf of ETA
edges.context: Community of Sant'Egidio offered to negotiate on behalf of ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: These were the first systematic demonstrations in the Basque Country against political violence. Also in 1986, in Ordizia, ETA gunned down María Dolores Katarain...
edges.context: ETA and GAL were adversaries in the Basque conflict, with GAL targeting ETA members and ETA attacking GAL-associated targets.

edges.evidence_quote: the law of parties was changed outlawing Herri Batasuna, the 'political arm' of ETA
edges.context: Herri Batasuna is described as the political arm of ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: in 2002 the Basque nationalist youth movement, Jarrai, was outlawed
edges.context: Jarrai was a youth movement associated with ETA, but the text does not specify the exact relationship beyond co-occurrence in being outlawed.

edges.evidence_quote: ETA cooperated with the Catalan nationalist movement, which had its own far-left separatist organization that based itself off ETA – Terra Lliure
edges.context: ETA cooperated with the Catalan nationalist movement, which included Terra Lliure.

edges.evidence_quote: Batasuna, the political party of ETA
edges.context: Batasuna is the political party of ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: ETA was known to have had 'fraternal' contacts with the Provisional Irish Republican Army; the two groups have both, at times, characterized their struggles as parallel.
edges.context: ETA and IRA had fraternal contacts and parallel struggles.

edges.target: Liga de Unidade e Ação Revolucionária
edges.evidence_quote: In the late 1960s, the Portuguese group Liga de Unidade e Ação Revolucionária (LUAR), which was fighting the dictatorship, brokered the contacts that allowed ETA to purchase weapons in the then Czechoslovak Republic.
edges.context: LUAR brokered weapons purchases for ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: ETA exchanged weapons, explosives and provided logistical support to the Forças Populares 25 de Abril (FP-25), a Portuguese far-left terrorist group.
edges.context: ETA exchanged weapons and provided logistical support to FP-25.

edges.evidence_quote: In 1999 ETA militants teamed up with the (now self-dissolved) Breton Revolutionary Army to steal explosives from magazines in Brittany.
edges.context: ETA and Breton Revolutionary Army teamed up to steal explosives.

edges.evidence_quote: ETA had extensive ties with the Breton Liberation Front – both organizations shared intelligence, and Breton revolutionaries received training from ETA in the Basque movement's clandestine camps in the Pyrenees.
edges.context: ETA shared intelligence and provided training to Breton Liberation Front.

edges.evidence_quote: A Catalan revolutionary organization that based itself off ETA, Terra Lliure, was an ally of ETA, and both organizations coordinated their actions and supported each other.
edges.context: Terra Lliure was an ally of ETA; they coordinated actions and supported each other.

edges.evidence_quote: The Colombian government stated that there are contacts between ETA and the Colombian guerrillas FARC. The recent capture of FARC's leaders' computers, and leaked email exchanges between both groups, show that ETA members received training from FARC.
edges.context: ETA had contacts with FARC; ETA members received training from FARC.

edges.evidence_quote: Italian author and mafia specialist Roberto Saviano pointed to a relationship of the group with the Mafia. According to this view, ETA trafficked cocaine which it got via its FARC contacts, then traded it with the Mafia for guns.
edges.context: ETA trafficked cocaine from FARC and traded it with the Mafia for guns.

edges.evidence_quote: A trial against a little-known organization called Xaki, acquitted in 2001 as the 'international network' of ETA.
edges.context: Xaki was described as ETA's international network.

edges.evidence_quote: A trial against the youths' movement Jarrai - Haika - Segi, accused of contributing to street violence in an organized form and connivance with ETA.
edges.context: Jarrai-Haika-Segi was accused of connivance with ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: A trial against the political organization Ekin (Action), accused of promoting civil disobedience.
edges.context: Ekin was accused of promoting civil disobedience in connection with ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: A trial against the organization Joxemi Zumalabe Fundazioa, which was once again accused of promoting civil disobedience.
edges.context: Joxemi Zumalabe Fundazioa was accused of promoting civil disobedience in connection with ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: A trial against the prisoner support movement Amnistiaren Aldeko Komiteak.
edges.context: Amnistiaren Aldeko Komiteak was a prisoner support movement linked to ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: A trial against Batasuna and the Herriko Tabernak (people's taverns), accused of acting as a network of meeting centres for members and supporters of ETA.
edges.context: Batasuna was accused of acting as a network of meeting centres for ETA members and supporters.

edges.evidence_quote: A trial against Batasuna and the Herriko Tabernak (people's taverns), accused of acting as a network of meeting centres for members and supporters of ETA.
edges.context: Herriko Tabernak were accused of acting as a network of meeting centres for ETA members and supporters.

edges.evidence_quote: A trial against the league of Basque-language academies AEK. The case was dropped in 2001.
edges.context: AEK was tried in connection with ETA but the case was dropped.

edges.evidence_quote: A trial against the association of Basque municipalities Udalbiltza.
edges.context: Udalbiltza was tried in connection with ETA.

edges.evidence_quote: Anti-ETA groups: Acción Nacional Española
edges.context: Acción Nacional Española was an anti-ETA group.

edges.evidence_quote: Anti-ETA groups: ATE (Anti-Terrorismo ETA)
edges.context: ATE was an anti-ETA group.

edges.evidence_quote: Anti-ETA groups: Batallón Vasco Español
edges.context: Batallón Vasco Español was an anti-ETA group.

edges.evidence_quote: Anti-ETA groups: Grupos Antiterroristas de Liberación (GAL)
edges.context: GAL was an anti-ETA group.

edges.evidence_quote: Anti-ETA groups: Guerrilleros de Cristo Rey
edges.context: Guerrilleros de Cristo Rey was an anti-ETA group.
